Roadmap for accelerated adoption of E-buses in India
11
E-Bus Research and Development
Problems with R&D in E-Buses
1.  Lack of R&D incentives: Currently, Indian OEMs encounter significant 
challenges in conducting R&D for electric buses, primarily due to the 
absence of government incentives for EV Aggregates (battery packs, 
electric motors, and controllers). R&D activities require substantial 
investments, placing a financial burden solely on the OEMs. Moreover, the production of E-
Bus components demands specialized equipment and technical expertise, which are 
currently lacking in India and necessitate considerable R&D and technology transfer efforts. 
2. R&D efforts lagging behind global counterparts: Indian companies also face a 
competitive disadvantage compared to their Chinese counterparts, who have 
advanced considerably within the EV ecosystem. In China, subsidies for EV industry 
started in term of R&D grants in 2009 using government initiatives like the "863 Program" 
that focused on improving technology in China at par with other developed countries. 
This discrepancy between India and China creates an uneven playing field for Indian 
OEMs in the R&D efforts. The limited availability of battery and motor testing facilities is 
further limiting the R&D progress of e-buses in India. 
Suggested way forward
Efforts have already been underway to improve the E-mobility R&D scenario in the country, 
especially with the recent E-Mobility R&D roadmap released by the Govt. of India. The State 
governments too have advanced their efforts to boost R&D investments. Karnataka has planned 
to set up an Electric Mobility Research and Innovation Centre, while Andhra Pradesh has 
committed INR 5 billion to support a Centre for Automotive Research and Mart Mobility. Bihar has 
plans to offer funding for companies that invest INR 200 Cr and generate at least 200 jobs. Other 
states should follow suit and include R&D as a key pillar in their EV policies. In order to further 
support the R&D scenario in the E-Bus sector, the government can implement several measures.
1. Tax deductions: One approach is to introduce tax deductions known as super 
deductions for R&D expenditure to help OEMs save on taxes and reduce financial 
burdens.
million public buses and 2.94 million private buses. Given the 2024 value of approx. 2.1 million 
buses registered in India, it is estimated that the bus fleet will be augmented by about ~1 million 
buses. Close to 40% of the currently operational fleet is likely to reach its end of life and will need 
to be replaced with new buses and 40% of the new buses sold is expected to be electric by 2030. 
However, to reach that stage, a concerted effort by all the E-Bus ecosystem stakeholders is 
required to address the challenges currently being faced and then move on the best path 
forward. The below section aims to articulate the same and suggest the roadmap for the 
Government and Industry stakeholders.


Roadmap for accelerated adoption of E-buses in India
12
2. Incentives for research on components other than Cells: R&D incentives / PLI schemes 
could also be provided for components other than Advanced Chemistry Cells as well as 
for investments in design tools, testing equipment, and prototypes to enhance OEMs' in-
house development capabilities. Financial support through soft loans or subsidies could 
facilitate technology transfer and knowledge acquisition efforts. Despite the PLI 
schemes, firms entering the EV component space will need to invest large amounts as 
upfront capital. It is imperative for government grants, international investors, and 
development agencies to channel direct green funds towards the EV component 
industry. The national-level PLI schemes should have some consideration for MSMEs and 
start-ups either by relaxing investment criteria or setting aside a specific allocation.
Impact
By incentivising research and development for e-buses, OEMs would be encouraged to enhance 
their innovation capabilities to develop cheap and technologically competent EV components 
that can compete with imported components further resulting in reduction in E-Bus selling 
prices. Support from the government in terms of establishment of testing labs will result in ease 
of product development eventually affecting the quality and performance of new e-buses. 
E-Bus Manufacturing
Problems with supply side policies
1.  O ver-reliance on foreign imports: Indian E-Bus OEMs face significant 
challenges due to a heavy reliance on foreign countries for 
components, thus being subject to unfair practices. Particularly, the 
reliance on China, for critical components keeps Indian companies 
exposed to fluctuating prices and supply uncertainties. The lack of deterrents for importing 
products that could be produced domestically further complicates the localization issue. 
More specifically, there are no effective measures in place to discourage the import of 
chassis. These factors collectively hinder the localization efforts of Indian OEMs in the 
electric bus sector, undermining their competitiveness and autonomy in the market.
2. Disjointed manufacturing policies: The policies that do apply on the market presently 
regarding the promotion of EV manufacturing are varied & disjointed in each state.  
Consolidation of policies with special additions that encourages investment in EV 
manufacturing is the need of the hour. 
Suggested way forward
Efforts to expand the supply capacity of E-Bus manufacturing are underway to reach the 
targeted 50,000 E-buses on roads by 2027 with the NEBP. State governments have also included 
supply-side interventions as a key part of their EV policies. Tamil Nadu, Andhra Pradesh, and 
Telangana, in particular, have announced significant supply-side interventions. Andhra Pradesh 
specifically, provides 25% capital subsidy for MSMEs and 35% on Plant & Machinery. All these 
states see the transition to electric mobility as an opportunity to redefine the traditional auto 


Roadmap for accelerated adoption of E-buses in India
13
cluster, by attracting International and Indian, newer manufacturers and component suppliers. 
The focus is on maximising manufacturing value addition and jobs from the EV transitions. 
However, over and above the efforts taken by the government and the industry, further support 
may be required
1. Uniform policies: A uniform policy to promote e-bus manufacturing could include 
measures such as subsidized land rates and exemption from stamp duty throughout the 
EV ecosystem, covering land purchases, leases, and agreements with state government 
entities. 
2. Fixed pricing on critical components: Fixed pricing for key imported components and 
materials like battery cells is crucial to stabilize costs and reduce reliance on international 
markets.
3. Improvement in manufacturing & supply chain processes: Government policy must 
account for the trade-offs associated with import substitution and the growth of EV 
manufacturing. Some improvements that Indian component suppliers will need to make, 
to compete with the best global products are (i) faster turnaround times (ii) automating 
the supply chain (iii) improving product consistency (iv) adapting to fulfil aftermarket 
demand.
Uniform policies: A uniform policy to promote e-bus manufacturing could include measures such 
as subsidized land rates and exemption from stamp duty throughout the EV ecosystem, 
covering land purchases, leases, and agreements with state government entities. 
Impact
High cost of finished goods can be minimized by fixing prices for imported components. Policies 
that encourage local manufacturing of components more vigorously will reduce the supply-side 
reliance on foreign counterparts, and thereby help in achieving economies of scale in the long 
run more sooner.
E-Bus Taxation
Problems with E-Bus taxation
1.  Differential taxation: Indian OEMs encounter several critical taxation 
challenges and one major issue is the inverted tax structure: while EVs 
themselves attract a GST rate of 5%, the raw materials, components, 
and spare parts used in their manufacturing are subject to GST rates 
ranging from 18% to 28%. This disparity leads to increased production costs and reduces the 
competitiveness of domestically manufactured electric buses. Furthermore, the absence of 
GST input tax credit increases the financial burden on OEMs, limiting their ability to offset 
taxes paid on inputs against their output tax liability.


Roadmap for accelerated adoption of E-buses in India
14
E-Bus Procurement – STUs
Problems with Central subsidies
1.  Complicated process for claiming subsidies: Securing funds through 
subsidies involve navigating complex and lengthy approval processes, 
which can be time-consuming. Strict guidelines and ambiguous 
documentation requirements further complicate the subsidy claiming 
process, leading to delays and inefficiencies. Moreover, the slow processing of subsidy 
claims adds to the financial strain and operational challenges faced by OEMs.
2.  Lack of subsidies for Private applications: The current structure of the FAME-II scheme is 
limited to providing subsidy benefits to STUs and government applications, excluding 
private sector applicants. This exclusion restricts potential market growth and adoption 
of e-buses among private inter-city operators, which constitute a significantly larger 
market with cost parity over diesel buses.
3.  Unstandardized tenders: Due to the demand aggregation model being followed since 
the Grand Challenge tender, there will be a need for multiple variants to fulfil each city's 
unique requirement, which leads to increased timeline for development, testing and 
production.
Suggested way forward
1. Faster subsidy claims processing: There is a critical need for faster turnaround times in 
subsidy claims processing. Implementing automated processes with minimal human 
intervention or exploring direct incentive transfers to customers can expedite fund 
disbursement and alleviate cash flow issues for OEMs. Establishing an OEM-friendly 
subsidy remittance scheme with transparent procedures is essential for fostering trust 
and compliance. Providing a clear checklist outlining subsidy requirements and process 
flows would streamline applications and enhance clarity for stakeholders. 
Suggested way forward
1. Output GST: The introduction of Output GST would enable OEMs/operators to claim 
Input GST benefits, thereby easing the financial strain caused by the inverted tax 
structure. 
2. Uniform GST: Implementing a uniform GST rate of 5% across the entire EV ecosystem, 
including specific aggregates such as batteries and charging infrastructure 
development will result in significant savings.
Impact
Lower GST rates on essential components and the introduction of output GST would reduce 
production costs for the OEMs and further promote the adoption of e-buses among private 
players. The loss in GST collection from EV components shall be recovered via the satisfaction of 
demand for E-Bus in the market.


Roadmap for accelerated adoption of E-buses in India
15
2. Subsidies extended to private sector: Extending subsidy benefits to private sector 
applications is crucial. Introducing proportional subsidies based on battery capacity, 
would incentivize private investments, reduce upfront costs, and significantly boost the 
adoption of E-buses in the private segment. The government can roll out specific 
programs to introduce E-buses in these operations within the new FAME scheme to boost 
electrification, and subsequently extend the existing subsidies to all private operators. 
Being less financially strained than SRTUs, private operators are less hindered by their 
financial position and have a longer period for assessing financial viability; thus, the 
future fuel cost savings from E-buses could be an attractive proposition for the private 
sector.
3. Standardized tenders: Standardization of models for all cities will not only facilitate larger 
participation of OEMs in tenders, but also improve the development & delivery timelines
Impact
In addition to retaining the FAME subsidy for the STU tenders, the FAME III scheme should also 
focus on extending the policy benefits to private sector players. This will compensate for the high 
upfront costs for the private operators and help enable e-bus adoption at a larger scale. This will 
further help in the overall development of e-bus ecosystem with influx in investments from 
various fronts.
E-Bus Procurement – Private Applications/Use-cases
Problems with achieving cost parity in private applications
1.  High TCO in private applications: Indian OEMs specializing in E-buses 
will encounter challenges related to TCO, particularly in segments such 
as school and employee transportation. These segments often have 
low usage in terms of kilometres driven and operational days, which 
adversely affects the financial feasibility of transitioning to an EV fleet. Compounding this 
issue is the absence of regulatory mandates requiring these segments to switch to electric 
buses within defined timelines.
Suggested way forward
In the United States, the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) has initiated the Clean 
School Bus Program, which allocates $5 billion over five years to replace 8,500 existing 
diesel school buses with zero-emission buses. Learning from this case, the Govt. of India 
can extend subsidies to the private segment.
1. Capital subsidies: Extending subsidies to include the private segment is crucial to 
incentivize investment in E-buses across diverse market sectors. Specifically, introducing 
capital subsidies tailored for school and employee transportation over the next five years 
can help offset higher initial costs associated with E-buses. 


Roadmap for accelerated adoption of E-buses in India
16
2. Targets set for private E-Bus penetration: Balancing these subsidies with regulatory 
targets for transitioning to electric vehicles would encourage accelerated adoption and 
market penetration. By aligning financial incentives with regulatory frameworks, the 
adoption of E-buses in segments with lower utilization rates can be enhanced.
Impact
A segment that consists as many numbers of buses as all the STUs combined will be led on a 
path of electrification. As the segment is already regulated and has safety protocols already 
imbibed, the transition will be smoother
E-Bus Financing
Problems with E-Bus Financing
Even after the application of capital or performance based 
subsidies, E-Buses are still an expensive affair due to its high 
acquisition costs. Ever since the success of the Grand Challenge 
tender, mass procurement has become mainstream leading to more significant impact 
on the balance sheets. However, the problems do not end here.
1.  High TCO in private applications: Indian OEMs specializing in E-buses will encounter 
challenges related to TCO, particularly in segments such as school and employee 
transportation. These segments often have low usage in terms of kilometres driven and 
operational days, which adversely affects the financial feasibility of transitioning to an 
EV fleet. Compounding this issue is the absence of regulatory mandates requiring these 
segments to switch to electric buses within defined timelines.
2.  Lack of green funds: The absence of dedicated Green Funds or long-term financing 
options further complicates the financial landscape for OEMs, hindering their ability to 
invest in and scale up E-bus production. Public and private investors in India and abroad 
are increasingly looking to finance green projects. Hitherto, most of this funding in the 
transport sector has gone to metros. Buses, especially E-buses, must be established as a 
potential avenue for this investment. Currently, the lack of profitability and inadequate 
professional capacity in STUs are the major barriers to attracting global green capital to 
the E-bus sector.
3.  Mandatory Bank Guarantees: A significant burden is also imposed by the requirement 
for Bank Guarantees (BGs), which restricts borrowing capacity and reduces available net 
debt for funding electric bus projects. Currently, BGs are mandatory for both Demand-
Based (covering 100% of subsidies with a tenure of 5 to 6 years) and Performance-Based 
(3% of total project cost for the contract duration) incentives, leading to substantial fund 
blocking.
4.  Limited availability of market financing: The absence of competitive market financing 
options further complicates matters, making it challenging for OEMs to secure 


Roadmap for accelerated adoption of E-buses in India
17
affordable and flexible funding. Disbursement mechanisms have issues, with delays in 
releasing funds—20% at Letter of Intent (LOI) signing, 40% on delivery, and 40% post-
operation—which disrupt cash flow management and project timelines. Furthermore, 
the lack of standardized rating systems for electric buses, relying on subjective 
interpretations of certifications like CMVR and ARAI, increases perceived risks among 
financiers and raises financing costs for OEMs, discouraging investment in electric bus 
projects.
5.  Mandatory OEM involvement: The requirement for a Letter of Undertaking from the 
parent company, pledging unconditional financial support, adds another layer of 
constraint on borrowing limits and operational flexibility.
Suggested way forward
1. Inclusion in Priority Sector Lending: Streamlining and expediting lending policies specific 
to e-buses is crucial to facilitate easier access to financing for OEMs. Implementing 
Priority Sector Lending (PSL) for e-buses would ensure access to affordable credit, 
considering the higher capex involved in manufacturing electric buses. Granting 
Infrastructure Status to the e-bus industry, particularly for fleet operations, would 
improve financing terms and attract more favourable lending conditions from financial 
institutions.
2. Funds from Government-owned NBFCs: The Central government must actively 
advocate for buses as a channel of investment, as it has done for metros. There is a 
strong case to be made for setting up a National Transport Development Finance 
Corporation, along the lines of some state governments, such as Tamil Nadu and Kerala. 
This body can disburse funds to the SRTUs and help in purchasing buses as well as 
paying for operational costs.
3. Performance based Bank Guarantees: Exploring alternative mechanisms such as surety 
bonds, currently under evaluation by the finance ministry, could replace Bank 
Guarantees to reduce the burden on OEMs and operators. If Bank Guarantees are to 
continue, it must be done for Performance-Based incentives. Performance Bank 
Guarantees should be structured with a proportionate reduction basis over the 
contract's life. Moreover, funding up to 85% with extended durations of 8 to 10 years at 
interest rates comparable to those for ICE vehicles is essential to support long-term 
investment in E-buses.
4. High upfront disbursements: Increasing upfront disbursements to 40% at LOI and 
delivery phases, with the remaining 20% post-operations, would streamline cash flow 
and project execution.
5. Rating agency for E-Buses: Establishing a central rating agency like CRISIL or CARE to 
evaluate E-buses as commercial vehicles rather than purely EVs would mitigate risk 
perceptions, reduce financing costs, and attract more investment.
6. Dedicated leasing ecosystem: Fostering a dedicated leasing ecosystem through 
Development Financial Institutions (DFIs) would facilitate easier access to financing and 


Roadmap for accelerated adoption of E-buses in India
18
support growth in the E-bus sector, and especially motivate the private bus operators to 
make the transition to an EV fleet
7. Reducing operational costs: Waiving RTO and toll charges for up to 7 years and providing 
state-owned lands at subsidized rates for charging, parking, and maintenance 
infrastructure near operational areas would significantly reduce operational costs. 
Maharashtra government in May 2025 extended motor vehicle tax and registration fee 
exemption benefit for EVs under the latest EV policy. To further promote zero-emission 
mobility in the state, the policy has made travel toll-free for buses on specific routes.
Impact
Implementing these financing policies for electric buses would significantly enhance access to 
affordable credit for OEMs and operators, facilitating the transition to e-buses. By streamlining 
lending processes, increasing upfront disbursements of funds, and establishing supportive 
financial mechanisms, operational costs would be reduced with an increase in cash flow, 
encouraging greater adoption of electric buses including the private sector.
E-Bus Workforce
Problems with the EV workforce
1. L ack of skilled drivers: Key issues include the inaccessibility of 
adequately skilled personnel, particularly skilled drivers for E-bus 
operations. The demand for experienced drivers exists for tender 
applications, yet the number of qualified drivers remains insufficient.
2. Lack of skilled technicians: There is also a shortage of EV technicians, essential for 
maintaining and repairing E-Buses. Reskilling initiatives for engineers and technicians 
from ICE backgrounds are necessary to meet the evolving demands of the EV industry. 
There will be a shift in demand for skills related to mechanical engineering to a 
combination of mechanical and electrical engineering. Demand will also increase for 
newer skillsets such as telematics, automation, AI, IoT, and data analytics.
Suggested way forward
The Chinese government has launched vocational training programs to upskill the 
workforce on the electric vehicle industry. In addition to this, the vocational education 
schools in partnership with private EV players like BYD have launched training institutes 
for hands on knowledge on electric vehicle development. Learning from examples from 
around the globe, India can create significant impact in the EV sector.
1.  Driver training schools: Establishing driver training schools under a PPP model, with 
state-wise mapping, can address the shortage of skilled drivers. Standardized training 
programs should be developed, eliminating language proficiency barriers to driver 
recruitment.


Roadmap for accelerated adoption of E-buses in India
19
2. Courses on the EV sector: Specialized courses focusing on EV manufacturing and 
maintenance should be integrated into existing educational curricula, including diploma 
program. Private institutes should be incentivized to establish such courses through 
concessions and sponsorships, facilitating the creation of a skilled workforce. Initiatives 
must be scaled up rapidly, with a clear roadmap that accounts for the availability of 
professionals as well as the skill requirements. This will necessitate significant 
collaboration between industry, academia, and government, at a level not seen before.
3. Industry-academia collaboration: An apprenticeship program, supported by OEMs and 
offering part cost offsets and tax benefits, would further enhance employment 
opportunities and skill development in the EV sector.
4. Training on the EV sector: Support from bodies like the Automotive Skills Development 
Council (ASDC) is crucial for upskilling mechanics to handle EV technologies effectively. 
The ASDC has also launched an electric mobility training programme for people with a 
background in engineering, and private auto OEMs have tied up with the ASDC to offer 
such courses. Government initiatives should also prioritize reskilling ICE engineers and 
technicians, with subsidies available to OEMs and operators for training programs. 
Educational and skilling institutions will need to offer easily accessible programmes that 
can create the skilled labour force needed in the evolving automobile industry. To this 
end, the Ministry of Skill Development and Entrepreneurship has announced a blueprint 
for creating 10 million jobs in the EV space. One of the key features of this programme is 
the creation of EV-specific occupational standards.
Impact
This approach will ensure a multidisciplinary workforce capable of meeting the diverse needs of 
the evolving EV ecosystem and adapting to technological advancements over time.
E-Bus Infrastructure
Problems with the E-Bus infrastructure
1.  Lack of charging infrastructure: One significant issue is the inadequate 
availability of charging infrastructure for inter-state transport, limiting 
the operational range and flexibility of E-buses. Captive charging 
stations built for intra city buses under the GCC model are often 
underutilized, primarily due to their exclusivity to specific OEMs and limited accessibility for 
other operators or private E-buses.
2. Lack of comprehensive charging policy: The lack of a comprehensive charging policy or 
guideline restricts the interoperability and shared usage of charging stations among 
different OEMs, operators, and private car owners. Technological limitations in charging 
infrastructure add to these challenges, alongside complex and lengthy approval 
processes for setting up new charging stations. Land allocation issues further 
complicate infrastructure development, with inadequate charging facilities along 


Roadmap for accelerated adoption of E-buses in India
20
highways increasing the accessibility issues for E-bus fleets. A major opportunity that is 
often overlooked is the underutilization of depots, the places where the charging infra is 
located, which are dedicated for government E-buses, thereby posing challenges for 
private operators.
3. Lack of subsidized commercial tariffs: The absence of subsidized commercial tariffs 
specifically designed for EV bus charging is a major issue regarding the tariffs paid for 
charging the E-buses. This lack of favourable tariff structures increases operational costs 
for operators, adding to the already strained operations. Various state governments and 
DISCOMs have not uniformly adopted central government advisories concerning 
demand charges and electricity duties. This disparity in adoption leads to inconsistent 
policies and additional compliance burdens for OEMs and operators, complicating the 
setup and operation of EV charging infrastructure across different regions.
Suggested way forward
1. R educing bottlenecks in charging infra approvals: Establishing a clear checklist outlining 
subsidy requirements and streamlined process flows is essential to facilitate smoother 
and faster approval and implementation of charging stations. 
2. Comprehensive charging policy: Introducing a uniform charging station policy and 
guidelines that allow private E-bus fleets to utilize government and public charging 
infrastructure will open STU's captive EV charging stations to the public during off-peak 
hours and would optimize their utilization and enhance accessibility for all EV operators. 
Access to bus charging infrastructure should be extended to any OEM operating E-buses 
within government fleets, promoting fair competition and maximizing resource use. This 
will also serve as an additional revenue generating opportunity for the STUs. There 
should be more initiatives to commercially monetize STU's real estate, thereby providing 
additional revenue to support them.
3. Uniform permits: It is important to adopt uniform policies across states concerning 
permits, approvals, passenger taxes, tolls, and permits for E-buses. Streamlining these 
processes would promote an environment for E-bus deployment across different regions.
4. Increasing accessibility of charging stations: Mandating the establishment of charging 
hubs in public parking lots and facilitating land sanction to private entities for setting up 
charging stations on lease or rental basis will significantly expand the charging 
infrastructure network. There is a critical need to enhance grid connectivity, particularly 
in city outskirts, to guarantee uninterrupted power supply for charging stations. 
Implementing express feeder lines can further ensure the delivery of high-quality 
electricity necessary for sustained operations of E-buses.
5. Unified roaming platform for charging solutions: Government intervention is crucial to 
mandate a unified roaming platform that integrates chargers from diverse Charge Point 
Operators and Mobility Service Solution Providers. This initiative, supported by 
recommendations from industry bodies like the Bus Operators Confederation of India 
(BOCI) and State Transport Undertakings (STUs), should prioritize strategic locations 
such as highways and key cities to increase e-bus adoption.


Roadmap for accelerated adoption of E-buses in India
21
E-Bus Operator settlement
Problems with the existing payment settlement setup
1. D elays in introducing Payment Security Mechanism: In the current 
mode of e-bus operations through the GCC PPP model in India, there 
are several challenges related to the payment of operators for the 
services provided due to the poor financial conditions of the STUs. 
Recently, through the PM e-bus Sewa scheme, a payment security mechanism – PSM was 
proposed to ensure performance-based payments to OEMs/operators. However, its cabinet 
approval has been delayed, creating financial uncertainties for stakeholders. While a central 
government-initiated Payment Security Mechanism promises some relief, its effectiveness is 
to be studied.
2. Shift from capex to opex subsidy: The scheme has also shifted from capital subsidies to 
an opex-based subsidy, calculated on kilometres travelled, which does not aid in lowering 
the initial acquisition costs of the buses. There are also no subsidies available for 
significant operational expenses such as chargers, depot construction etc. placing 
further financial strain on operators.
3. Difficulty in enforcing the escrow: The establishment of an escrow account was also 
stipulated in the scheme to manage payments securely, but there have been consistent 
issues with its enforcement, notably the timely deposits into the account.
Suggested way forward to solve operator payment settlement issues
To address the challenges in the e-bus operations, several recommendations can be 
made.
1.  Fast track PSM: The government needs to fast track the cabinet approval for the 
Payment Security Mechanism to provide better financial security and facilitate smoother 
deployment and funding for bus operations.
6. Private sector involvement in depot infrastructure: Providing Capex subsidies and other 
financial incentives to private applications, like Delhi's Premium Bus services, can 
stimulate private sector involvement. Enhancing the quality of infrastructure at depots 
and customer boarding points for private operators is also essential. 
7. Clarity on battery swapping: Govt. has been working on E-Bus battery swapping norms 
as having common battery swapping stations for all the e-buses in a city has been 
determined as the best pathway for reducing infrastructure requirements and improving 
output. However, the govt. needs to provide clarity on how the battery swapping fits into 
the existing schemes & E-Bus policies to retain OEM & Operator confidence.
Impact
Improving the accessibility of charging infra to E-Bus operators in Depots, Highways, 
Rural/Outskirt locations will boost operator confidence for investing in the route.


Roadmap for accelerated adoption of E-buses in India
22
2. Re-introduction of Capex subsidy: Reintroducing a CAPEX subsidy tied directly to vehicle 
costs, as was done during the FAME-I & II schemes, would significantly help reduce the 
acquisition burden for operators.
3. Follow escrow protocols: It is also recommended that STUs be mandated to maintain 
and timely fund their escrow accounts, with penalties for non-compliance. The control of 
escrow accounts should rest with the bidders to ensure financial transparency and 
security.
4. Introduce state government PSMs: State governments should introduce their own 
Payment Security Mechanisms, similar to the central government's, and extend these to 
existing contracts to enhance financial stability across the board.
5. Increase fares: STUs should have a periodical fare revision mechanism to account for the 
increase in fuel prices and staff costs. This will allow for more tariffs that are competitive 
and encourage private buses to enter the organised bus system on a contractual basis. 
Alternative models involving some level of control over fare box revenues to private 
operators must be implemented for specific operations, such as creating an alternate 
premium bus service alongside the broader public bus system.
6. Dedicated regulator: The establishment of a dedicated regulator for the electric vehicle 
sector is recommended. This regulator would function similarly to the Telecom 
Regulatory Authority of India (TRAI) and the Central/State Electricity Regulatory 
Commissions (CERC/SERC), providing a unified approach to policy-making and 
implementation.
Impact
Solving the current challenges related to operator payment settlement is a multipronged effort. 
It requires addressing the regulatory hurdles, following of operating procedures, and increased 
revenue collection to be able to run the E-Bus operations smoothly.


Roadmap for accelerated adoption of E-buses in India
23
Conclusion
To pave the way for a robust E-Bus ecosystem in India, the government must take decisive 
actions to address the outlined challenges. A comprehensive policy framework with streamlined 
approval processes is an absolute necessity to encourage domestic manufacturing, R&D and E-
Bus infrastructure. Government must facilitate easier financing options by granting 
infrastructure status to the E-Bus industry and promoting green funds. Faster processing of 
subsidies, extending benefits to private operators, and creating a dedicated EV regulator will 
also streamline operations and enhance market growth.
On the industry's side, OEMs and component manufacturers need to scale up their capabilities 
by investing in cost-reducing technologies. Leveraging state and central subsidies effectively, 
optimizing supply chains, and prioritizing local manufacturing will be crucial to improve the cost 
parity. Enhancing workforce skills through specialized training programs and collaborating with 
educational institutions can address the talent gap. The industry must also advocate for uniform 
policies across states and actively participate in creating a sustainable, scalable E-Bus 
ecosystem. By aligning efforts with governmental initiatives, the industry can effectively 
contribute to the widespread adoption of E-buses, and ensure the future of public 
transportation is secure.


Roadmap for accelerated adoption of E-buses in India
28

TOWARDS SUSTAINABLE MOBILITY

To reduce the costs… and thereby Govt. support
